food_name: Asam Pedas Ikan (Sour Spicy Fish Stew)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Asam Pedas Ikan. Closest parent is tamarind-based spiced fish stew. No exact URI available; gap noted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish stew in tamarind-based spiced broth (Malay asam pedas style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FRG used as closest defensible parent for fish stew category; no exact Asam Pedas Ikan code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Asam Pedas Ikan or equivalent Malay fish stew.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Asam Pedas Ikan is one bowl containing fish pieces and broth, approximately 300g, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Asam Pedas Ikan — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Asam Pedas Ikan, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for validation. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish-Based Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Protein-Dominant Seafood Dishes; HPB Singapore Dietary Reference
gi_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore; University of Sydney GI Database (analogous reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Asam Pedas Ikan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the dish's very low carbohydrate content (4.2g/100g), (2) high protein content from fish which blunts glycaemic response, (3) tamarind acidity which slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose rise, and (4) comparison with similar low-carbohydrate fish-based stews in the HPB Singapore dietary reference framework. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (40 × 4.2 × 3) / 100 = 5.0g; per 100g = (40 × 4.2) / 100 = 1.7. Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 520mg per 100g; a 300g serving delivers approximately 1,560mg sodium (65% of the 2,400mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200g and request reduced belacan and salt during preparation, as a standard 300g serving contributes approximately 1,560mg sodium — a significant proportion of the recommended daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.2g/100g) and low glycemic index (estimated GI 40); high fish protein supports insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Asam Pedas Ikan is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a standalone dish, given its negligible carbohydrate load (4.2g/100g), estimated low GI of 40, and high-quality fish protein; glycaemic impact is primarily determined by the accompanying steamed rice rather than the stew itself.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Marine fish provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA); low saturated fat (0.8g/100g); anti-inflammatory spice base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The marine fish base of Asam Pedas Ikan provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), and the dish's low saturated fat content (0.8g/100g) and anti-inflammatory spice ingredients (turmeric, galangal, lemongrass) support cardiovascular health, though sodium intake should be monitored in patients with heart failure or hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from marine fish and anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric, galangal, and lemongrass
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Regular consumption of Asam Pedas Ikan may benefit patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from marine fish and bioactive compounds in the spice base including curcumin from turmeric and gingerols from galangal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asam Pedas Ikan is a quintessential Malay sour-spicy fish stew that represents one of the most beloved and iconic flavour profiles in Malay cuisine across Singapore and Malaysia. The name translates literally as 'Sour Spicy Fish' — 'asam' meaning sour (derived from tamarind) and 'pedas' meaning spicy (from dried chilies). The dish is prepared by simmering fish — most commonly stingray (ikan pari), mackerel (ikan kembung), or red snapper — in a richly aromatic broth made from tamarind water, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, dried chili, and toasted belacan (fermented shrimp paste). The signature ingredient is torch ginger flower (bunga kantan), which imparts a distinctive floral-spicy aroma unique to this dish. Laksa leaves (daun kesum) further enhance the herbal complexity. Unlike coconut milk-based Indian fish curries, Asam Pedas Ikan has a clear, sour, oil-light broth with a vibrant red-orange colour from the chili and turmeric. Vegetables such as tomato and okra (lady's fingers) are commonly added. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein (12.5g/100g), low-carbohydrate (4.2g/100g), low-fat (3.2g/100g) dish with an estimated glycemic index of 40 (low). It is an excellent source of marine omega-3 fatty acids, selenium (22mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.8mcg/100g), and niacin (4.2mg/100g). Sodium content is moderate-to-high at 520mg/100g, primarily from belacan and salt. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is typically served hot with steamed white rice at Malay family meals, hawker centres, and Malay restaurants throughout Singapore and Malaysia. A standard serving is approximately 300g (one bowl of fish and broth). Clinically, it is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease due to its low glycaemic impact and omega-3 content, but sodium intake should be monitored for hypertensive patients.
common_user_queries: is asam pedas good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved regional source) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (approved regional source). Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas source policy. The dish is well-documented in both databases. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.7) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate based on the dish's low carbohydrate content, high protein from marine fish, and tamarind acidity; no direct GI measurement for Asam Pedas Ikan exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300g serving = 5.0g (low). Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per HPB source for this fish-based stew with no hydrogenated fats. Copper (0.1mg/100g), manganese (0.15mg/100g), and vitamin K (5.0mcg/100g) values are from enriched_json source with confidence 0.9; these are not mapped to per_100g template fields as they fall outside the standard template nutrient set but are preserved in source metadata. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by HPB source; set to -1. Water content not reported; set to -1. Hindi local name 'असम पेदास इकान' is a transliteration as no established Hindi culinary term exists for this Malay dish. The dish is a Singaporean Malay dish; multilingual naming requirements for English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are satisfied.

Converted flat aliases array of objects to flat string array as required by template.

Renamed 'diabetes' condition to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name='Type 2 Diabetes'.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 1.7 per 100g (GL = GI × carb_g / 100 = 40 × 4.2 / 100).
